title: Using a queen excluder correctly
subtitle: Where to put it, when to remove it, and why it matters.
excerpt: A queen excluder keeps the laying queen in the brood box and out of the honey supers above it. Fit it in the wrong place, leave it on at the wrong time of year, or neglect it and the bees will find ways around it, or you'll block the colony's expansion when it matters most.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024). BBKA exam syllabus material on hive management. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metal or plastic grid placed between the brood box and the honey supers; worker bees pass through it freely but the larger queen cannot.
A shallower box placed above the brood box, intended to receive surplus honey from the colony.

content.text: sits between the brood box and the first

. Workers slip through; the queen's larger thorax stops her. Get the timing right and the supers stay honey-only. Fit it wrong, or leave it on when you shouldn't, and you create problems, most commonly, a colony that refuses to move up through it and builds a honey barrier at the top of the brood box instead.

Fit the excluder once the colony is expanding well in spring and you're adding the first super for the season. Don't fit it too early: a colony that hasn't yet expanded into its brood box needs the flexibility of the full space. Adding an excluder before the colony is filling the brood box discourages upward movement.
In the UK the right moment is late April to mid-May. By then the colony fills most of its brood box and the first nectar flow has started.
Remove the crown board. Check there is no queen already in the super from a previous inspection, if she is above the excluder when you fit it, she'll be trapped there.
Lay the excluder flat on top of the brood box, making sure it covers the full opening with no gaps at the corners or sides. A poorly fitted excluder is one the queen can walk around.
Set the super on top of the excluder. If this is the first super of the year, add one or two drawn frames from last season in the centre to encourage the bees up.

Remove the excluder at the end of the honey season, once the supers are cleared and taken off. The colony should overwinter on the brood box only, with no excluder in place. A colony trying to cluster across an excluder will split the cluster, the queen and part of the workers on one side, the rest on the other, and the queen's half will starve.

After removing it at the end of the season, scrape propolis and wax from the grid with a hive tool. Propolis narrows the slots over time and eventually makes the excluder impenetrable to workers as well as the queen. A heavily built-up excluder can be soaked in warm water and scrubbed with a stiff brush. Check the frame or wire is not bent or buckled, even a small gap is enough for a small-thoraxed queen to squeeze through.

Wire (zinc or stainless) excluders last longer and are easier to clean than plastic slotted ones. Plastic ones are cheaper and less likely to injure bees' legs on the grid edges, but they become brittle with age and the slots can distort. Both types work; the choice is personal preference and budget.
